vol_description: UCR's data collection for the Play project.

vol_name: Freeviewing gaze behavior in infants, children, and adults
vol_description: Infants and children (6 months to 10 years) watched seven 2-minute videos while their eye movements were recorded. Data were also collected from college-aged adults. This excerpt volume shows the seven stimuli used and exemplar data from the youngest and oldest participants.

vol_name: Coordination of eyes and head in outdoor walking and search
vol_description: Adults and children walked along a walkway in a college campus and completed a search and retrieval task in a large courtyard to study how eye and head movements are coordinated across different tasks.

vol_name: Head and body structure infants’ visual experiences during mobile, naturalistic play
vol_description:
This is a secondary analysis based on the dataset: Adolph, K. & Franchak, J. (2015). See and be seen: Infant-caregiver social looking during locomotor free play. Databrary. Retrieved October 28, 2020 from http://doi.org/10.17910/B7.135. 

The new analyses build on the existing Datavyu coding files to add a measure of the distance to each object. Hand coding of object locations in the field of view produced a measure of visual centering for each object that appears in the final analysis files.

vol_name: Excerpt Volume: Classifying infant body position across the day using wearable inertial sensors
vol_description:
This is an example of one participant's data and corresponding analysis code. The data include full-day inertial sensor timeseries for ankle and hip-worn sensors, video recordings from the beginning of the day, and Datavyu coding files of infant body position for each video. The linked coding example illustrates how the video-coded data were linked to the IMU data, and how the resulting synchronized data were used for machine learning classification. 

Note, the Datavyu coding files included 6 coding categories (u = upright, p = prone, hs = held by caregiver, l = supine, sr = sitting restrained, and ss = sitting on a surface), but the two sitting categories were collapsed into a single category in analysis.

There are two videos (pt1 and pt2). Part 1 corresponds to the "proximal comparison period" described in the paper, during which we had tight synchronization between sensors and video. During the video, the experimenter arrived at the house, synchronized the sensors (9:11am) and left the equipment for the caregiver. The caregiver brought the video camera into the house and put the sensor garment on the infant at 9:29 am. Part 2 corresponds to the "distal comparison period" during which the videos were only coarsely synchronized with sensor data. Part 2 follows Part 1 by a temporal gap of roughly 40 seconds. The IMU sensors continued to record even after the end of the part 2 video, until the caregiver removed them at 5:46 PM. The IMU files have data both before and after the period that the infant was wearing them; the accompanying code synchronized and cleans the data to capture the usable segment.

vol_name: Inertial Sensing and Language Recording of Infants across a Day
vol_description:
Infants in two cohorts (4, 5, 6, 7 months) and (11, 12, 13, 14 months) participated in 1 or multiple sessions. 
At each session, infants wore inertial movement units on both ankles and hips to train machine learning classifiers of movement behavior. Infants wore a LENA audio recorder. Parents wore inertial movement units on their wrists and hips to record movements. The first 1-3 hours were recorded by a stationary video camera, and all other sensors recorded until infants' bed time.
